steps.text: Conduct testing to ensure that sampling techniques written for a specific organism are effective for additional organisms.
Validate to ensure that techniques tested for collecting samples for one type of assay will perform with equal proficiency with the proposed assays.
Test samples collected from multiple locations and under differing conditions in conjunction with matrix spikes and proficiency evaluation testing.
Troubleshoot and optimize the sampling technique after conducting additional validation studies.
Repeat some or all aspects of the validation study.
Document the process for selecting a location representative of the analyte(s) distribution in the local environment.
List and understand the mission requirements.
Review and answer additional questions to clarify sampling technique requirements.
Use the decision tree in Figure 1 to choose the appropriate sampling technique to validate for a given purpose.
Define and reconcile parameters, their performance criteria, and the conditions under which they may be tested based on their programmatic components.
Consider acceptable trade-off conditions in advance of multiple parameter optimization.
Develop optimization parameters, data ranges, test conditions, and trade-offs in consultation with, and accepted by, the user community.
Document decisions regarding optimization parameters, data ranges, test conditions, and trade-offs.
Provide the user community with information that occurs during the optimization process.
List operational limits in the sampling technique descriptions.
Include known operational limits in the documentation.
Describe testing conditions so that potential users can assess whether test conditions accurately replicate potential difficulties.

Incorporate all technique performance components, including personnel, laboratory facilities, reagents, supplies, equipment, calibrations, reporting standards, record keeping, data analysis, safety, and quality.

Evaluate the selected sampling technique carefully based on project goals, matrix, location of microbes, heterogeneity, sampling device, the target itself, exposure to sunlight and introduction of dissolved oxygen.
Select surrogates.
Add ('over-spike') a control organism or compound into a sample in precise numbers as a specimen-processing control (SPC) or to understand the effects of treatment, antibiotics, etc.
Consider the ability to capture the surrogate in a manner similar to the target when selecting a sampling technique.
Consider the potential impact that sample collection and transportation might have on the parameter of interest.
Specify sample collection areas within the collection parameters.
Specify the volume or mass of the sample.
Review SOPs annually and make modifications as necessary.
Maintain proper and adequate records and files, including sample numbering and tracking, analytical data, reports, and QC data.
Electronically scan or otherwise convert hard copy records to an electronic format.
Back up electronic records regularly.
Document reviews and audits formally.
Make documented reviews and audits available to persons responsible for the work.
Correct deviations from required standards as soon as possible.
Check chemical preservatives for efficacy.
Use recommended preservation methods for all collected samples.
Ensure sample bottles contain sufficient sodium thiosulfate to neutralize the residual chlorine present.
Evaluate sample integrity elements, including temperature control.
Package, label, and ship potentially hazardous materials according to the appropriate ground or air regulations.
Store samples or wastes in a secure area protected from vermin and adverse weather conditions.
Separate hazardous and nonhazardous samples and wastes.
Prepare a written description of the technique.
Include numerical and descriptive specifications of the technique's operational limits and performance attributes in the description.

preconditions.text: Prior to using sampling techniques written for a specific organism for additional organisms (even when the microbes are closely related).
Prior to using techniques tested for collecting samples for one type of assay with proposed assays.
Sampling technique difficulties are revealed after performing multiple tests
An acceptable range of response variables must be provided prior to technique optimization.
The target organism is low in prevalence and highly pathogenic, or the surrogate is an indicator for the sanitary condition of the water rather than a specific pathogen.
If a split or replicate of a sample is to be sent to a laboratory for analysis
If samples are not collected from a surface area
Samples are collected from chlorinated water sources and the target analyte is a viable organism

constraints.text: Address safety in both the sampling technique and the validation plan, including personal protective equipment and first aid.
Address physical and biological hazards such as harmful microorganisms.
Sampling technique options often are mandated by regulatory constraints.
Developers should not promise delivery on performance criteria during the parameter-formulation period, as this might influence parameter selection and bias outcomes.
Holding time and temperature can affect microbiological samples, potentially causing either an artificial increase or decrease in the number and viability of target organisms.
Field conditions such as meteorological conditions, low light levels, the absence of horizontal surfaces, or the requirement to wear protective gear may limit the performance of a technique.
Decisions regarding performance components must be based on the impact that these factors will have on the technique used, the data quality, and whether or not the data are acceptable for use.

Ensure that the properties of the control(s) are similar to those of the target organisms especially with regard to sampling (i.e., is the interaction of the surrogate with the matrix and sampling device the same as that of the target?).
Air incorporated into a water sample during collection may change the pH of the sample from an anoxic environment.
Ensure that records are stored securely and can be retrieved easily.
QA system audits should be independent, thorough and preferably unannounced.
Samplers and analysts must be vigilant in preventing contamination of samples and reagents.
All hazardous goods shipments must comply with the regulations and guidance described above.
Personnel with approved DOT/IATA training must perform all shipments of potentially hazardous materials.
Sample and packaging integrity must be maintained to ensure safe shipment.
Shipments may be subject to the Select Agent Rules (42 CFR 72.6).
